title: Generalizing Verifiable Instruction Following
authors: Valentina Pyatkin, Saumya Malik, Victoria Graf, Hamish Ivison, Shengyi Huang, Pradeep Dasigi, Nathan Lambert, Hannaneh Hajishirzi (Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence; University of Washington)
venue: NeurIPS 2025, Datasets & Benchmarks Track (per arXiv page comments); arXiv preprint 2507.02833, v3 2025-11-11
claim: Most current LLMs, including strong frontier models (GPT-4.1, Claude 3.7/4 Sonnet, Qwen3-32B), strongly overfit to the small, saturated set of 25 verifiable constraints in IFEval and fail to generalize to unseen verifiable constraints, scoring below 50% on the new IFBench benchmark (58 OOD constraints) despite scoring 80%+ on IFEval. The paper further shows that reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (IF-RLVR / GRPO), especially when trained on many stacked constraints per instance and a large/diverse constraint-type taxonomy, significantly improves both in-domain (IFEval) and out-of-domain (IFBench) instruction-following performance.
novelty: The authors present three contributions as novel versus prior work: (1) IFBench, a new benchmark of 58 unseen, diverse, human-authored verifiable constraints (paired with WildChat prompts never released for training) specifically designed to measure OOD generalization rather than in-domain memorization, since IFEval's 25 constraints have saturated; (2) IFTrain, 29 new hand-annotated training constraints + verifier functions that more than double the existing training-constraint pool, enabling systematic study of how constraint diversity/count affects generalization; (3) IF-RLVR, new RLVR training recipes -- notably training on combinations of up to 5-6 stacked constraints per prompt, and mixing verifiable with preference-model rewards to counter reward hacking/over-optimization -- which the authors position against the only other automatically-verifiable-training-data work they identify (VFF, which used SFT/DPO, not RLVR) (Section 6 Related Work).

protocol.description: IFBench (and IFEval) evaluation defines two settings, both single forward-generation per turn (Section 2): (1) Single-turn: the model receives one 'user' prompt that concatenates a general task t with one or two output constraints c, and must produce a response satisfying t while adhering to c in a single completion. (2) Multi-turn: the constraint is isolated across three conversation turns -- turn 1 is a 'user' prompt with just task t, turn 2 is a synthetic/assistant response r1 to t, and turn 3 is a 'user' message asking the model to rewrite r1 to comply with constraint c; the model responds to turn 3 given all prior turns as context (i.e., one generation call, but with dialogue history as demos/context rather than task+constraint co-present from the start). Accuracy is computed both 'strict' (constraint-following function run directly on model output) and 'loose' (output first cleaned by stripping first/last lines and certain font/markdown modifiers before verification) -- mirroring IFEval's own strict/loose split (Section 2, 'IFBench' subsection). No worked examples/demos are shown to the model in-prompt beyond the constraint's natural-language template; verification is fully code-based post-hoc, not visible to the model. The paper's own LLM-as-judge auxiliary protocol (Appendix C) is a separate, non-verifiable side-analysis, not part of the main IFBench scoring: 'Evaluate the response provided below to determine if it meets the specified constraints... Provide an integer score from 1 to 10... The evaluation must be structured in the following JSON format: "Score": "<An integer score from 1 to 10.>"'.
protocol.observation_content: Per the quoted protocol text, in the single-turn setting the model sees exactly one message: a general task prompt t concatenated with the instantiated constraint text(s) c (e.g., 'Maintain a 2:1 ratio of declarative to interrogative sentences.'); no chain-of-thought scaffolding, few-shot demonstrations, or partial/prior feedback are described as part of the base evaluation protocol. In the multi-turn setting the model sees three turns of conversation history (task prompt, a prior response r1, and a follow-up user request to revise r1 for constraint c) before generating its single reply -- this is exposure to more context/an intermediate artifact, not iterative feedback on the model's own attempts. Average input length: single-turn IFBench prompts average 76 tokens; multi-turn conversations average 408 tokens of input (prompt+response+prompt) (Appendix D.1).

representations.summary: The paper does not compare alternative input/output serialization formats for the same constraint content (e.g., JSON vs. natural language) as an experimental axis for accuracy. The representation-like variables it does study are: (a) single- vs multi-turn placement of the constraint (Section 2, Section 4.8/Table 7); (b) number of constraints stacked per training/eval instance, 1 through 6+ (Section 4.1, Table 1, Figure 2-3); (c) variable-range instantiation strategy for constraint parameters -- 'different range' (disjoint train/test), 'wider range' (train range extends/includes test range), 'same range' (Section 4.3, Figure 5); (d) constraint category composition via leave-one-out ablation over IFEval's 9 categories (change cases, detectable format, length constraints, keywords, etc.) (Section 4.4, Figure 6). Findings: training on a 'wider range' of constraint-variable values performs comparably to or better than training on the exact test range, while training on a fully disjoint ('different') range consistently underperforms (Section 4.3) -- i.e., diversity/coverage of the instantiation space matters more than an exact format match. Multi-turn-trained models improve multi-turn accuracy but do not reach comparable single-turn performance unless trained on a mix of both (Table 7, Section 4.8).

models.name: Frontier out-of-the-box models (GPT-4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Claude 4 Sonnet, Qwen3-32B, o3, Gemini, Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct, Tulu3-70B)
strategies: zero-shot single-turn prompting (no CoT/few-shot scaffolding described)
models.key_results: Score below 50% on IFBench while scoring 80%+ on IFEval (Section 1 Introduction, Figure 1); o3 is the only model IF-RLVR-trained models do not outperform (Section 3.1).

models.name: Llama-3.1-Tulu-3-8B (SFT / DPO / final Tulu / IF-RLVR variants)
strategies: GRPO-based RLVR (IF-RLVR) starting from SFT or DPO checkpoint; comparison to DPO training on same verifiable-preference data
models.key_results: IFEval improves from 82.4 (Tulu, Table 3) / 81.1 (Tulu-DPO) to 92.2 after IF-RLVR (Tulu-DPO base); IFBench improves from 28.9 (Tulu) / 25.5 (Tulu-DPO) to 44.6 after IF-RLVR (Table 3, Section 1 contributions, Table 6). DPO-vs-GRPO controlled comparison (Table 5): GRPO after DPO reaches IFEval strict 89.65 / IFBench strict 30.6, vs DPO after DPO at IFEval strict 79.67 / IFBench strict 29.3.

models.name: Qwen2.5-7B (base and instruct)
strategies: IF-RLVR from base with reasoning chat template (App. F); IF-RLVR from instruct checkpoint; single-turn vs multi-turn vs mixed RLVR training (Section 4.8)
models.key_results: IF-RLVR raises Qwen2.5-7B (base) to IFEval 87.8 and IFBench 54.7 per abstract-adjacent contributions list (Section 1); Table 6 reports IF-RLVR from base Qwen2.5 at IFEval 87.8 / IFBench 53.7, and instruct Qwen2.5 before IF-RLVR at IFEval 74.7 / IFBench 31.3 rising to IFEval 89.1 / IFBench 45.9 after. Table 7 (multi-turn ablation): Qwen2.5-7B trained on single-turn data scores single-turn IFE./IFB. 79.9/55.8 but only 57.4/50.0 on multi-turn; trained on a mix reaches 85.2/51.0 single-turn and 62.5/51.0 multi-turn (best balance).

models.name: OLMo2 (base and instruct)
strategies: IF-RLVR from base with reasoning chat template; IF-RLVR from instruct
models.key_results: Table 6: instruct OLMo2 before IF-RLVR IFEval 61.7 / IFBench 16.7, after IF-RLVR IFEval 74.5 / IFBench 44.6; from-base OLMo2 after IF-RLVR reaches IFEval 70.4 / IFBench 46.6.

models.name: Llama-3.1-8B (base)
strategies: IF-RLVR from base with reasoning chat template
models.key_results: Table 6: from-base llama3.1 after IF-RLVR reaches IFEval 88.2 / IFBench 54.1 (base pre-RLVR not evaluable, no chat template).

models.name: GPT4.1 (as LLM-as-judge, not a candidate/generation model)
strategies: LLM-as-judge scoring rubric, Appendix C, used only for auxiliary response-quality analysis
models.key_results: Base-policy completions score avg 7/10; IF-RLVR-trained completions score avg 6.4/10 on the same prompts with constraints removed (Section 5, 'Trade-offs between response quality and instruction following').

headline_numbers: Frontier models score <50% on IFBench (58 new OOD constraints) vs 80%+ on IFEval's 25 constraints (Section 1 Introduction; Figure 1).

IF-RLVR raises Tulu-3-8B: IFEval 82.4 -> 92.2, IFBench 28.9 -> 45.9 per the paper's own Section 1 contributions-list sentence, but Table 3 and Table 6 both report 44.6 for this same IF-RLVR Tulu-DPO configuration (45.9 in Table 6 is actually the instruct-Qwen2.5 IF-RLVR IFBench score) -- an inconsistency within the paper itself, not an extraction error; treat 44.6 as the table-verified figure for Tulu.
IF-RLVR raises Qwen2.5-7B base: IFEval to 87.8, IFBench to 54.7 (Section 1 contributions list; cf. Table 6's 53.7 for the from-base run).
Table 1: training on 3 stacked constraints per instance gives best IFBench (59.5) among the 1-6 sweep; IFEval peaks at 6 constraints (85.8) (Section 4.1).

Table 2 breakdown: IF-RLVR (qwen2.5) reaches per-category scores case 93.3, detect.format 94.4, keywords 93.2, length 90.9, count 67.7, format 68.1, words 67.0, sentence 71.1 -- IFEval categories largely saturate (>90) while IFBench-style words/sentence categories remain low (Section 4.5).

Table 4 (DPO preference-pair construction): only Qwen-72B gets 'all correct' on >1/4 of instances (26%), and even it gets 100% 'only one wrong'; most models are far worse (Tulu-3-70B 15% all-correct, Llama3-8b 6%, Yi-34B-Chat 10%) (Section 4.6).
Table 5 controlled GRPO vs DPO: GRPO after DPO achieves IFEval strict 89.65 / IFBench strict 30.6 vs DPO after DPO's 79.67 / 29.3 (Section 4.6).

Table 7 multi-turn ablation: models trained only on single-turn data lose ~20-30 points on multi-turn IFEval when evaluated multi-turn (Qwen2.5-7B: 79.9 single-turn vs 57.4 multi-turn); mixed training narrows this gap (Section 4.8).
failure_modes: Overfitting/non-generalization: models saturate the 25 IFEval templates but fail to generalize to the 58 unseen IFBench constraint types, with the same task/eval setup (Section 2, Abstract).

Reward hacking / instruction-hierarchy conflict: IF-RLVR-trained models over-prioritize the verifiable constraint at the expense of overall task quality/helpfulness -- example given: 'write a recipe for tiramisu' + 'only use unique words' constraint, where RLVR models prioritize the constraint over producing an actual usable recipe (Section 1, Section 5, Figure 7). Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct in particular 'tends to prioritize the constraint over the instruction' and its IF accuracy is 'the most negatively correlated with LLM-as-judge scores' among analyzed models (Section 5).

Quality/constraint-following trade-off measured directly: base-policy completions score higher on LLM-as-judge quality (7/10 avg) than IF-RLVR completions (6.4/10 avg) on the same prompts with constraints stripped, even though IF-RLVR completions have higher verifiable accuracy (Section 5).

Training instability from unfavorable variable ranges: training on a constraint-variable range disjoint from the test range ('different range') consistently underperforms same-range or wider-range training (Section 4.3, Figure 5).

Diminishing/non-monotonic returns from constraint count in training: Table 1 shows IFBench performance is not monotonic across n=1..6+ stacked training constraints (peaks at 3, dips at 4, recovers at 5, dips again at 6; further dips at higher counts like 10, 50, 1000), indicating the stacking-count dial does not simply help more with more constraints.
Base-model policies without post-training cannot even be evaluated pre-RLVR (no chat template) -- a methodological/coverage gap noted directly in Table 6's caption.

AlpacaEval 2 (general instruction quality) degrades under IF-RLVR training even as IFEval/IFBench improve, e.g., Tulu-DPO's AlpacaEval2 33.5 -> IF-RLVR Tulu-DPO's 21.3; Qwen2.5 base's IF-RLVR run scores only 1.1 on AlpacaEval2, 15.3 on GSM8K, showing collateral damage to non-IF skills when RLVR is not blended with other reward signals (Table 3, Section 4.5).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper's core evaluations are single-forward-pass generations in both its 'single-turn' and 'multi-turn' settings -- there is no iterative feedback loop where the model retries after seeing its own verifier output during evaluation; 'multi-turn' instead means the constraint is presented as the final turn of a fixed 3-turn dialogue history (task, a response, a rewrite request), still resolved in one generation call (Section 2). Where the paper does draw a one-shot-vs-more-exposure distinction is in TRAINING: (1) stacking more constraints per training instance (up to 5-6) improves generalization to both IFEval and IFBench relative to training on 1-3 constraints (Section 4.1, Table 1); (2) training on a wider variety/range of constraint instantiations (more demonstrations of the same constraint template across varied parameter values) generalizes better than narrow or mismatched ranges (Section 4.3); (3) explicit multi-turn-formatted RLVR training data is needed to perform well on the multi-turn eval setting -- single-turn-only training transfers poorly to multi-turn, and only training on a mix of both formats yields models competitive on both single-turn and multi-turn accuracy (Table 7, Section 4.8). So the paper's conclusion is: a single forward pass is what's evaluated throughout, but what the model can do in that single pass is heavily determined by whether its training exposed it to the relevant constraint stacking depth, variable range, and turn-structure -- generalization to unseen combinations/depths/formats is the paper's central negative finding, not an ability that improves via in-context multi-step interaction at eval time (no evaluation-time chain-of-thought or multi-attempt protocol is described in the extracted text for the main results; reasoning models are handled only via post-hoc reasoning-chain stripping, Appendix G).

relation_to_candidate.position: IFBench sits on the single-shot end of the family for its primary ('single-turn') setting: one prompt containing task + 1-2 concatenated constraints, one generation, scored by running each constraint's Python verifier against the raw/cleaned output (strict/loose). It also defines a second, still single-generation-per-eval 'multi-turn' variant where the constraint is deferred to a third dialogue turn and the model must revise a prior response, which is a different observation surface (more context, an artifact to edit) rather than an iterative multi-turn feedback loop with per-turn scoring. Output shape in both settings is a full free-text response (not a single fact/label) that must simultaneously satisfy 1-2 composed constraints; verification is 100% deterministic/code-based (constraint-specific checker functions), never LLM-as-judge for the primary metric (LLM-as-judge is used only for an auxiliary quality-rating side-study, Appendix C/Section 5). Scoring in the base benchmark is per-constraint pass/fail rolled up typically over just 1-2 constraints per instance (not the 3-5-atom stack c22 proposes), though the paper's IF-RLVR training data does stack up to 5-6 constraints per instance -- that stacking-depth practice exists in the paper's training pipeline, not in the IFBench eval set itself, whose instances 'contain either one or two constraints' (Section 2).
relation_to_candidate.overlap_with_c22: Direct structural overlap: (1) deterministic, code-verified, per-constraint 0/1 checking -- exactly c22's checker-library approach; IFBench explicitly supplies verifier functions plus 58 new constraint templates across 7 categories (count, ratio, words, sentence, format, custom, copy) that are the natural OOD atom pool c22 draws from, per the corpus claim and c22's own 'Academic anchor' section. (2) The paper's own training recipe already stacks multiple (1-6) constraints per instance and finds this improves generalization (Section 4.1) -- directly validating c22's central design bet that stacked composed constraints are harder and more diagnostic than single atoms. (3) Both use a strict/loose or strict all-pass framing; c22's '0/1, no partial credit over the whole stack' is essentially IFBench's strict per-instance accuracy generalized to more atoms per instance. (4) Appendix A's constraint list includes several atoms that are exactly what c22 names in its worked example (word-count-range, keyword count/position, casing via title_case, end/start-with-token style constraints like first_word/last_word_answer in Appendix B, forbidden-word-like exclude_word_harder).
relation_to_candidate.differences: IFBench's released benchmark instances carry only 1-2 constraints per instance (Section 2), not c22's proposed 3-5; the higher stacking counts in the paper appear only in IF-RLVR *training* data construction (up to 5-6), not in the frozen 300-prompt IFBench eval set, so c22's harder 3-5-constraint eval stacking is a genuine extension beyond what IFBench evaluates. IFBench has no explicit 'Selection'/conditional constraint type ('if property P then rule A else rule B') in its 58 documented constraints (Appendix A) or its 29 training constraints (Appendix B) -- conditional/discover-not-copy constraints are not part of this paper's taxonomy at all; that mechanism is sourced elsewhere in c22's lineage (ComplexBench, per c22.md's own citation). IFBench's task is built by pairing a constraint with a substantive open-ended WildChat/SFT prompt (e.g., summarization, chat-style tasks), not a deliberately trivial micro-task ('name a color') as c22 proposes -- IFBench's t is realistic-length free text (avg 76 tokens for single-turn prompts, Appendix D.1), whereas c22 wants a minimal base task purely to isolate constraint-following signal from task-completion signal. IFBench does not log a full per-checker diagnostic verdict as a first-class benchmark artifact for downstream optimizer consumption (the paper reports category-level aggregate breakdowns in Table 2, not per-instance per-atom logs) -- c22's 'per-checker verdicts as diagnostic facts' is an added scoring/logging feature the paper does not describe.
relation_to_candidate.adds_beyond: c22 would add: (a) evaluation-time (not just training-time) stacking of 3-5 constraints per instance, going beyond IFBench's shipped 1-2-constraint eval instances into a harder regime the paper only explored for training data; (b) a Selection/conditional-constraint mechanism entirely absent from IFBench's 58+29 constraint taxonomy, requiring the model to discover a hidden branch condition rather than just satisfy stated atoms -- a qualitatively different, ComplexBench-style composition operator; (c) a deliberately trivial/minimal base task so that failures are attributable to constraint-following alone, versus IFBench's substantive real-world prompts where task-quality and constraint-following interact (the paper's own Section 5 shows this interaction causes reward hacking/instruction-hierarchy trade-offs, a confound c22's design tries to sidestep); (d) per-checker diagnostic verdict logging as a structured optimizer-facing signal, beyond IFBench's category-level aggregate reporting; (e) a synthetic, infinite/procedurally-generated instance pool (per c22.md, COLLIE-style grammar) versus IFBench's fixed, human-annotated, 300-prompt frozen release.

corpus_claim: IFBench / IF-RLVR (Pyatkin et al., 2025) supplies 58 OOD verifiable constraints plus verifier functions -- c22's non-one-shot-guessable OOD atoms -- and is GEPA's headline instruction-following task.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The paper fully and verbatim supports the '58 OOD verifiable constraints plus verifier functions' portion: 'We introduce a new benchmark, IFBench, to evaluate precise instruction following generalization on 58 new, diverse, and challenging verifiable out-of-domain constraints' (Abstract), and 'A new, unseen and challenging precise instruction following benchmark, IFBench, with 58 new constraints and corresponding verification functions' (Section 1, contribution 1). The extracted paper text, however, contains NO mention of 'GEPA' anywhere in the body, abstract, related work, or appendices -- that clause of the corpus claim cannot be verified from this source and should be marked unverified-against-this-paper (it is presumably sourced from a separate GEPA paper/dossier, not from 2507.02833 itself). The corpus claim's phrase 'c22's non-one-shot-guessable OOD atoms' is the corpus's own framing/attribution, not a claim made by the IFBench paper (IFBench does not describe its constraints as designed to defeat 'one-shot guessing' -- its own framing is about generalization/overfitting on a fixed constraint set, not one-shot enumerability by a prompt-optimizer). Recommend correcting the corpus claim to note the GEPA linkage is unverified from this paper and is presumably attested elsewhere.
baseline_relevant: Constraint count per instance in the released eval set: IFBench instances contain either 1 or 2 constraints (Section 2, Figure 1 caption) -- useful as a floor/reference difficulty dial; c22's 3-5-constraint stack is deliberately harder than the shipped benchmark.

Training-time stacking sweep (Table 1, Section 4.1): n in {1,2,3,4,5,6,10,50,100,500,1000} constraints per training instance; IFBench score peaks at n=3 (59.5) and is non-monotonic thereafter (49.4 at n=4, 55.8 at n=5, 54.1 at n=6, falling to ~48-52 at higher n) -- suggests a moderate stacking depth (3-5) is a reasonable, empirically-motivated sweet spot for c22's own difficulty dial, and that pushing far beyond 5-6 constraints does not straightforwardly help (in this paper's training-data context, not eval).

Metrics: prompt-level strict and loose accuracy (Section 2/Section 3, 'Experimental & Evaluation Setup': 'We report the prompt-level loose accuracy for both IFEval and IFBench'); strict = raw verifier on raw output, loose = verifier on output after removing first/last lines and font modifiers.

Floor observed: frontier out-of-the-box models score below 50% on IFBench vs 80%+ on IFEval (Section 1); IF-RLVR-trained small (7-8B) open models reach ~44-55% IFBench after training (Table 3, Table 6) -- gives a rough sense of where an un-optimized vs optimized baseline should land for a 58-OOD-constraint-style task, and that even substantial RLVR training leaves IFBench well below IFEval-level accuracy (ceiling not near-saturated at 7-8B scale).

Category-level floors (Table 2): even after targeted IF-RLVR training, 'words' and 'sentence' categories remain far lower (48.1-67.0 and 36.7-71.1 respectively across the two IF-RLVR-trained models, tulu3DPO-IF-RLVR and qwen2.5-IF-RLVR; the pre-RLVR tulu3DPO baseline scores even lower, 7.0 words / 13.3 sentence) than 'change cases'/'detectable format' categories (82.9-99.2 across all three models, near-saturated) -- signals that categories closer to c22's proposed atoms (word-level manipulation, sentence-level constraints) are intrinsically harder and slower to saturate than simple casing/format atoms.

Contamination/data-release status: IFBench prompts are drawn from WildChat prompts 'held out from release' specifically to prevent train-test contamination, and all 58 eval + 29 train constraints were manually/human-authored to guarantee no overlap between train and test constraint sets (Section 2, Appendix D.2: 'As all constraints were human-written, we could ensure that there is no overlap between the two evaluation sets'). Code for IFBench is released at https://github.com/allenai/IFBench (Section 1, footnote 2), and IFEval-taxonomy training data is noted as 'Apache 2.0 license' (Section 3, Data).

Prompt-format effects: variable-range mismatch between train and test (Section 4.3) is the one 'representation'-adjacent factor shown to swing accuracy substantially -- training on a disjoint ('different') range of constraint parameter values underperforms training on the same or a wider range, i.e., c22's generator should sample constraint parameters (e.g., word counts, letter targets) from a range that generalizes rather than one narrowly matched to a fixed evaluation range.

Length/scale facts for prompt design: single-turn IFBench prompts average 76 tokens; frontier off-the-shelf models produce ~2214-token average responses on IFBench, while IF-RLVR-trained models produce much shorter ~210-token average responses (Appendix D.1) -- relevant if c22 wants to compare response-length behavior as a diagnostic of over-optimization to short/constraint-satisfying but low-effort outputs.
quotes: "We find that most models strongly overfit on a small set of verifiable constraints from the benchmarks that test these abilities, a skill called precise instruction following, and are not able to generalize well to unseen output constraints." (Abstract)
"We introduce a new benchmark, IFBench, to evaluate precise instruction following generalization on 58 new, diverse, and challenging verifiable out-of-domain constraints." (Abstract)

"consists of 58 new verifiable constraints that go beyond the 25 constraints included in IFEval... Every instance went through a human annotation process to verify the prompt and constraint compatibility... The final benchmark consists of 300 prompts... Each instruction in the benchmark contains either one or two constraints." (Section 2, 'IFBench' subsection)

"Single-turn: The 'user' prompt consist of a general prompt with task t, concatenated with one or more output constraints c, and the model has to complete t while adhering to c." / "Multi-turn: c is isolated from t in three turns... The model has to respond to the third turn, given all previous turns as context." (Section 2)
"leading LMs such as Qwen3-32B or Claude 4 Sonnet score below 50% showcasing the opportunity for improvement in precise IF." (Section 1, contribution 1)
"With our new training techniques we improve the IFeval scores of a Tülu-3-8B model from 82.4 to 92.2, and the IFBench scores from 28.9 to 45.9." (Section 1, contribution 3)

"Removing the constraints from the length and the keywords categories harms IFEval performance the most, while removing constraints from the change cases and detectable format categories barely affect performance with the model achieving an IFEval accuracy of 89.65" (Section 4.4)
"As all constraints were human-written, we could ensure that there is no overlap between the two evaluation sets." (Appendix D.2, Constraint Diversity)

corrections: baseline_relevant Table 2 category-floor claim: 'words' range (48.1-67.0) and 'sentence' range (13.3-71.1) were computed inconsistently -- 'words' excluded the tulu3DPO pre-RLVR baseline value (7.0) while 'sentence' included its baseline value (13.3). Fixed to report both IF-RLVR-trained-model-only ranges (words 48.1-67.0, sentence 36.7-71.1) plus the excluded pre-RLVR baseline values (7.0 words / 13.3 sentence) explicitly, so the comparison is apples-to-apples and the omission is no longer silent.

headline_numbers item 2 (Tulu-3-8B IFBench 28.9 -> 45.9): flagged that this 45.9 figure, while a verbatim quote from the paper's own Section 1 contributions-list sentence, conflicts with Table 3 and Table 6, which both report 44.6 for the IF-RLVR Tulu-DPO configuration; the paper's Table 6 shows 45.9 is actually the instruct-Qwen2.5 IF-RLVR IFBench score, not Tulu's. This is an inconsistency in the source paper itself (not an extraction artifact), now made explicit in the dossier rather than left as an unexplained conflicting figure.
